# 🎯 Overview & Spec

# Tiered Prefix Cache well-lit path in Prism

***Public/externally accessible***  
Contributors: [Sean Horgan](mailto:seanhorgan@google.com), [Nishtha Jain](mailto:nishjain@google.com) [Rajitha Leonhard](mailto:rajithal@google.com) , [Danna Wang](mailto:dannawang@google.com), \<add your name\>  
Apr 29, 2026

# Overview

The KV Cache's massive accelerator memory footprint is a primary bottleneck in LLM serving, directly constraining context length, concurrency, and overall system throughput. Prefix Cache Offloading (Tiered Prefix Cache) solves this by extending the KV cache from expensive GPU/TPU HBM to larger, cost-effective tiers like CPU RAM, Local SSDs, and remote storage layers (Managed Lustre). Integrating this into Prism as a well-lit path allows customers to easily visualize and adopt these optimizations, maximizing their cache hit ratios and token/s throughput and unlocking long-context serving on cost-effective hardware like G4s without hitting memory limits. This also allows customers to visualize their efficiency gains (through TTFT, token/s, ITL metrics) and therefore, allow them to utilize their hardware (TPUs or GPUs) to the max.

## Business Impact Metrics

* **Performance Gains (TTFT, Token/s throughput, ITL):** Measured directly via `inference-perf` benchmark tool and stored in the llm-d Results Store GCS bucket.  
* **Cost-to-Serve (Cost per 1M Tokens):** By increasing throughput and enabling long-context workloads to run on cheaper G4/RTX-PRO-6000 instances (rather than forcing an upgrade to less available & more expensive machines with A3/H100s), the cost per token decreases dramatically. We will measure this by calculating the hardware hourly rate divided by the improved token throughput. As an example, we [already have proven TCO gains](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/storage-data-transfer/choosing-google-cloud-managed-lustre-for-your-external-kv-cache?e=48754805) with KVCache offloading to Managed Lustre.  
* **Feature Adoption Rate:** We need to track how often customers are exploring and adopting this well-lit path. We will measure this by leveraging the existing Google Analytics in Prism to track page views, configuration toggles, and clicks on the reproducibility action links. We still need to figure out how we associate activity in prism with downstream engagement in cloud layer, e.g. provisioning infra.

# Jobs To Be Done (CUJs)

## CUJ 1: Evaluate Tiered Storage Performance

* **User Role:** Stack Operator (AI/ML Infrastructure Engineer)  
* **Goal:** Understand the performance trade-offs (Time to First Token, Throughput) of offloading KV cache to CPU RAM or Local SSD compared to an HBM-only baseline for large context workloads.  
  * Note: we should include workloads where it does not make sense to deploy kv cache offloading, e.g. kv cache \<\< HBM   
* **Pain Points:** Running manual benchmarks across multiple storage tiers and context lengths is incredibly time-consuming, error-prone, and difficult to reproduce.

## CUJ 2: Compare these results to my configuration

* **User Role**: Stack Operator (AI/ML Infrastructure Engineer)  
* **Goal:** Understand the price/performance difference between the configuration benchmarked in the guide and my custom configuration which will use different models, machines, and workloads.  
* **Pain Points:** Knowledge of the detail configuration in the benchmark, e.g. specific vLLM version & kernels 

Some ideas brainstormed between Sean & Rajitha

* Use AI to prepopulate a FAQ for common comparisons germane to this guide. E.g. how would gemma4-31B perform under the same configuration  
* Make it easier to share the benchmark details with an agent  
* Embed AI/agent directly in the UX

Brainstorm during sync-up

* Direct user to a deploy planner app. Here’s the source: [llm-d-planner](https://github.com/llm-d-incubation/llm-d-planner) 

## CUJ 3: Reproduce Tiered Prefix Benchmarks

* **User Role:** AI Platform Architect  
* **Goal:** Validate published benchmarks using their exact workloads and infra.  
* **Pain Points:** Strict, smaller HBM limits on G4 hardware severely restrict the number of concurrent long-context requests that can be served, traditionally forcing an expensive hardware scale-up to A3/H100 clusters.

# Detailed Specifications

General flow

* **Data Source & Pipeline:** The `inference-perf` (run with llm-d-benchmark) tool will generate standardized output containing all the results data.   
* **Results Publication:** Establish a pipeline (Staging Bucket \-\> Review \-\> Production) to run combinations of models, accelerators, and storage tiers and publish them to the llm-d results store GCS bucket.  
* **Prism UI/UX:** The dashboard will feature dropdown menus allowing users to select between baseline, CPU offload, and CPU+storage optimization options. There will not be options to select between other options like different models, machine types in the initial release.  
* **Metrics Extraction:** The backend must parse the llm-d benchmark output to extract Input Token Throughput (tokens/s), Time to First Token (TTFT), and End-to-End Latency.

---

## Benchmarks

Public list here: [llm-d benchmark backlog \[External\]](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1_c5OVpa195HG-SbTB6uTaJ49B2OQFLFnUBiXDiv9nDc/edit?gid=0#gid=0)  
![][image1]

## VisualizationPrefix cache offloadingPrism Guide Details: Prefix Cache Offloading

We’ll ship the guide specified below as the “Prefix Cache Offloading” well-lit path experience in Prism:  
![][image2]

**Guide Overview** This guide demonstrates how to extend the KV Cache from HBM to other cost-effective tiers (CPU RAM, Local SSDs and Managed Lustre) using KV plug-ins (LMCache, tpu-inference and FS-connectors). By enabling tiered prefix caching, you can serve larger context windows and increase concurrency on cost-effective G4 instances, drastically improving throughput and TTFT for long-prompt workloads that would otherwise OOM.

**Selectable List of Optimizations**

* Baseline (HBM Only)  
* Tiered Cache: CPU RAM ([View llm-d CPU Offloading Guide](https://llm-d.ai/docs/guide/Installation/tiered-prefix-cache/cpu))  
* Tiered Cache: CPU RAM \+ Managed Lustre

Future options / maybe list as “Contact Us or Contribute”

* Tiered Cache: Local SSD  
* Tiered Cache: Cloud Storage (e.g. GCS Rapid Cache)

**Benchmark Scenario**

* **Infra layer:** GCP: `g4-standard-384` (8x `nvidia-rtx-pro-6000`).  
* **Model serving layer:** `google/gemma4-31B` (FP8), Parallelism Strategy: TP: 8, Model serving engine: vLLM (with FS-connector).  
* **Workload:** `inference-perf` (migrated from SGLang `bench_serving`), Use case: `generated-shared-prefix`, Input sequence lengths: 10k, 50k, 100k tokens.

**Primary Outcomes**

1. **Input Token Throughput (tokens/s):** Highlights the massive throughput gains when serving long contexts, especially where the baseline fails.  
2. **Time to First Token (TTFT):** Highlights the latency reduction achieved by avoiding recomputation of large prefixes.  
3. **Inter Token Latency (ITL):** Highlights the time elapsed between generating consecutive tokens, useful for measuring how effectively the cache is managed, especially in agentic workloads.

**Action** \*[Reproduce this benchmark with llm-d](https://llm-d.ai/docs/guide/Installation/tiered-prefix-cache/cpu)

**Key Charts**

* **Throughput vs. Context Length (Bar Chart):** Compares Input Token Throughput across Baseline, CPU RAM, and CPU RAM \+ LSSD for 10k, 50k, and 100k prompt lengths. *(Will visually highlight where Baseline hits OOM).*  
* **TTFT Reduction (Bar Chart):** Compares TTFT across the three storage configurations, highlighting the dramatic drop in latency for 50k and 100k prompts when using Local SSD.  
* **ITL Reduction (Bar Chart):** Compares ITL across the three storage configurations, highlighting the dramatic drop in latency for 50k and 100k prompts when using Local SSD.

**Summary Metrics Comparison (Illustrative for G4 / Gemma4-31B)** *(Note: Exact numbers to be dynamically pulled from the GCS result store upon completion of the G4 automated runs. The table below illustrates the expected narrative where G4's smaller VRAM causes early OOMs on the baseline).*

| Metric | Baseline (HBM Only) | Tiered Cache (CPU RAM) | Tiered Cache (CPU RAM \+ Lustre) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Throughput (10k tokens)** | *\[Baseline tok/s\]* | *\[+X% tok/s\]* | **\[+Y% tok/s\]** |
| **Throughput (50k tokens)** | OOM (Exceeds G4 VRAM) | *\[Recovered tok/s\]* | **\[Maximized tok/s\]** |
| **TTFT (10k tokens)** | *\[Baseline ms\]* | *\[-X% ms\]* | **\[-Y% ms\]** |
| **TTFT (50k tokens)** | OOM (Exceeds G4 VRAM) | *\[Recovered ms\]* | **\[Minimized ms\]** |
| **ITL (10k tokens)** | *\[Baseline ms\]* | *\[-X% ms\]* | **\[-Y% ms\]** |
| **ITL (50k tokens)** | OOM (Exceeds G4 VRAM) | *\[Recovered ms\]* | **\[Minimized ms\]** |

---

# 📈 Visualization options

# Visualizations

[Sean Horgan](mailto:seanhorgan@google.com), May 7, 2026  
Note: I fed in my concerns about bar charts and references to 3P guides 

Based on the benchmark data from the LMCache GKE blog and the Managed Lustre blog, you are absolutely right to question the use of simple bar charts. Bar charts only display static aggregates (like mean Time to First Token at a fixed request rate) and completely fail to capture the dynamic relationship between system load, saturation points, and the "memory wall" where Out of Memory (OOM) errors occur.

# Options

To better highlight how Tiered Prefix Caching impacts various throughput and latency load stages, here are four alternative visualization techniques tailored for the Prism Guide UX.

Recommendation for the Prism UX Flow:

1. **Primary View:** Lead with the **Latency vs. Throughput Curve** for a heavy workload (e.g., 50k tokens). This immediately proves the elasticity and scale of the tiered architecture.  
2. **Secondary View:** Provide the **Context Length Heatmap** as an interactive "Deploy Planner" tool (tying into CUJ 2). Let the user find their expected context length on the heatmap to see which tier they should toggle on.

## 1\. Latency vs. Throughput Curves

**Best for replacing:** *Throughput vs. Context Length Bar Charts*

* **How it works:** The X-axis represents System Load / Input Token Throughput (tokens/s), and the Y-axis represents Latency (TTFT or ITL). You plot a separate curve for each configuration (Baseline HBM, CPU RAM, CPU RAM \+ Lustre/Local SSD).  
* **Why it’s better:** This is the gold standard for performance engineering. It visually demonstrates the exact load stage where the Baseline hits a memory wall (OOM) or where latency exponentially spikes due to saturation. Users will clearly see the Tiered Cache curves extending much further to the right (handling higher throughput) while keeping the latency curve flat and stable.  
* **Data Application:** The Managed Lustre blog notes a 75% throughput improvement for 50k contexts. This plot would show the Baseline curve spiking and terminating early, while the Lustre curve continues to push high tokens/s before its latency degrades.

## 2\. Heatmap: Context Length vs. Storage Tier "Sweet Spots"

**Best for replacing:** *Summary Metrics Comparison Tables*

* **How it works:** Create a matrix where the X-axis is Context Length (1k, 5k, 10k, 50k, 100k) and the Y-axis is the Storage Configuration. The color intensity of each cell represents the % improvement in Throughput or TTFT.  
* **Why it’s better:** Grouped bar charts get incredibly cluttered when comparing 3 configurations across 5 context lengths. A heatmap makes the ROI instantly obvious.  
* **Data Application:** Looking at the LMCache GKE benchmark, HBM alone is fine for 1k tokens (0% change). CPU RAM becomes the sweet spot at 5k-10k tokens (up to \+50% throughput). Local SSD/Lustre absolutely dominates at 50k-100k tokens (+264% throughput). A heatmap visually guides the user to the exact architecture they need based on their prompt size.

## 3\. Latency CDF (Cumulative Distribution Function) Plots

**Best for replacing:** *TTFT / ITL Reduction Bar Charts*

* **How it works:** The X-axis is Latency in milliseconds (TTFT or ITL), and the Y-axis is the Percentile of Requests (0% to 100%).  
* **Why it’s better:** A bar chart only shows the *average* latency reduction. A CDF plot shows the *entire distribution*, proving that Tiered Caching doesn't just improve the mean—it drastically reduces **tail latency**. It visually proves to the user that offloading prevents the severe, 10-second worst-case latency spikes caused by recomputing massive prefixes.

## 4\. Stacked Area Chart: Prefill Time Composition

**Best for replacing:** *General TTFT Bar Charts*

* **How it works:** A single bar or area chart for a single request (e.g., 100k tokens) broken down by where the time is spent: *HBM Fetch Time*, *CPU RAM Fetch Time*, *Storage (Lustre/SSD) Fetch Time*, and *Compute (FLOPs) Time*.  
* **Why it’s better:** It tells the *story* of the optimization. Users need to understand that reading a 100k KV Cache from Managed Lustre over the network is actually significantly faster than forcing the GPU to recompute those 100k tokens from scratch. This visualization visually shrinks the massive "Compute Time" block from the baseline and replaces it with a much smaller "Storage I/O" block.

## Recommendation for the Prism UX Flow:

3. **Primary View:** Lead with the **Latency vs. Throughput Curve** for a heavy workload (e.g., 50k tokens). This immediately proves the elasticity and scale of the tiered architecture.  
4. **Secondary View:** Provide the **Context Length Heatmap** as an interactive "Deploy Planner" tool (tying into CUJ 2). Let the user find their expected context length on the heatmap to see which tier they should toggle on.

# Chart Axes for Cache Offloading

Offloading is fundamentally different in what it is trying to prove, but which variables align with each axis depends on whether you want to prioritize industry-standard performance visualization or strict UI consistency within Prism.

Here is a breakdown of why the axes differ, how the story changes if you flip them, and a recommendation on how to proceed.

### Why the Axes Differ: Saturation vs. SLA

**1\. The "Hockey Stick" (X \= Throughput, Y \= Latency)**

* **Used for:** Capacity, Saturation, and Stress Testing.  
* **The Logic:** Throughput (load) is the *independent variable*—it is what you are pushing into the system. Latency is the *dependent variable*—it is the system's reaction.  
* **Why it fits Offloading:** The primary story of Tiered Prefix Caching is the **Memory Wall**. You want to show that as you push more tokens/s (moving right on the X-axis), the Baseline HBM suddenly hits a wall (OOM) and the line abruptly stops or shoots straight up. Meanwhile, the CPU/Lustre lines continue smoothly to the right.

**2\. The "Plateau" or "SLA Curve" (X \= Latency, Y \= Throughput)**

* **Used for:** SLA-driven routing and Cost/Performance trade-offs.  
* **The Logic:** In routing, Latency is often treated as the *constraint* (e.g., "I have a strict 500ms TTFT budget"). Putting Latency on the X-axis allows the user to pick their SLA on the bottom, look up, and see how much Throughput (or cost savings) they can achieve at that specific latency.  
* **Why it fits Intelligent Routing:** Routing is about finding the most efficient path *within a given latency budget*.

### What happens if we flip the Offloading graph to match Routing?

If we maintain Prism consistency and use **X \= Latency, Y \= Throughput**, the graph rotates 90 degrees.

* Instead of a hockey stick, you get a **plateau**.  
* The line goes straight up (throughput increases while latency stays near zero), and then sharply bends to the right (throughput maxes out, and latency stretches out to infinity).  
* **The OOM Problem:** An Out-of-Memory error is harder to visualize here. The Baseline line would simply have a lower "ceiling" (lower max Y-value) than the Tiered Cache lines. It looks like a standard throughput bottleneck, rather than a catastrophic memory failure.

### Recommendation

**Recommendation: Keep X \= Throughput and Y \= Latency for the Tiered Cache Guide.**

Here is why:

1. **The "OOM" Story:** The primary value proposition of this specific guide (especially on G4 instances) is preventing OOMs on long-context workloads. The traditional hockey stick (X=Throughput, Y=Latency) is the only way to visually demonstrate a system crashing under load while the optimized system survives.  
2. **Industry Standard:** AI/ML Infrastructure Engineers (your target persona for CUJ 1 and 2\) are deeply accustomed to seeing load testing results with Load/Throughput on the X-axis.

**How to handle the Prism consistency issue:** If the Prism design team mandates that all charts must share the same axes, you can safely flip it to X=Latency, Y=Throughput. However, if you do this, you **must add visual annotations** (like a red "X" or a shaded "OOM Zone") at the top of the Baseline curve to explicitly show that the system didn't just slow down—it crashed because it ran out of VRAM.

# Chart Controls

Based on your notes and the core principle of progressive disclosure, the goal is to avoid the "dashboard of 15 dropdowns" while still allowing the user to manipulate the key independent variables (Workload Size, Storage Tier) to see the impact on the dependent variables (SLOs, Cost).

Since the initial release spec states that Model and Machine Type will be fixed (Gemma4-31B on G4 instances), we can lock those as contextual badges and focus the interactive controls entirely on the offloading strategy.

Here is a proposed UX layout and control scheme for the chart section, utilizing decision branching and progressive disclosure.

## 🗺️ Proposed UX Layout: The Chart Control Panel

**\[Context Badges \- Static for Initial Release\]** `Hardware: g4-standard-384` | `Model: gemma4-31B (FP8)` | `Engine: vLLM`

---

## 🎛️ Primary Controls (Always Visible)

*These controls directly manipulate the Latency vs. Throughput "Hockey Stick" chart.*

**1\. Workload Size (Context Length)**

* **UI Element:** Segmented Button / Toggle Bar  
* **Options:** `[ 10k Tokens ]` | `[ 50k Tokens ]` | `[ 100k Tokens ]`  
* **Rationale:** Addresses your note on workload size. By forcing the user to select one context length at a time, the chart remains clean. When they click "50k", they will instantly see the Baseline curve hit the OOM wall, while the offload curves continue.

**2\. Storage Tier Comparison (Independent Variable)**

* **UI Element:** Color-coded Checkboxes (Max 3-4 options)  
* **Options:**  
  * ☑️ Baseline (HBM Only)  
  * ☑️ Tiered Cache: CPU RAM  
  * ☑️ Tiered Cache: CPU RAM \+ Managed Lustre  
* **Rationale:** Allows users to overlay the specific strategies they are evaluating.

**3\. Target Metric (Dependent Variable / Y-Axis)**

* **UI Element:** Dropdown or Tabs above the chart  
* **Options:** `Time to First Token (TTFT)` | `Inter-Token Latency (ITL)`  
* **Rationale:** Keeps the chart to a single Y-axis to avoid visual clutter, letting the user switch between prefill (TTFT) and decode (ITL) performance.

## 🪄 Progressive Disclosure: "Cost & SLO Analysis" (Expandable Section)

*Hidden behind a toggle or "Advanced Options" chevron. This addresses your notes on Cost and SLOs without overwhelming the first-time viewer.*

**↳ Enable SLO Target Line**

* **UI Element:** Checkbox \+ Number Input  
* **Interaction:** `[x] Show SLA Limit:` `[ 500 ]` `ms`  
* **Rationale:** Draws a horizontal dashed line across the chart at 500ms. The user can visually trace where each storage tier's curve intersects this line, instantly answering: *"At my 500ms SLO, what is the maximum throughput I can achieve with CPU RAM vs. Lustre?"*

**↳ View Cost-to-Serve (TCO)**

* **UI Element:** Toggle Switch  
* **Interaction:** `[ View Performance ]` ⟷ `[ View Cost-to-Serve ]`  
* **Rationale:** Addresses your note that offloading isn't free. Flipping this toggle changes the Y-axis from *Latency (ms)* to *Cost per 1M Tokens ($)*. The backend calculates this by taking the hourly cost of the G4 instance \+ the hourly cost of the Managed Lustre capacity, divided by the throughput at that load stage.

---

## 🧠 How this maps to your Key Criteria

1. **Workload Sizes (Individual & Aggregate):**  
   * *Individual prompt size* is handled by the **Context Length** toggle (10k/50k/100k).  
   * *Aggregate system workload* is inherently handled by the chart's **X-Axis (Throughput in tokens/s)**. As you move right on the chart, you are simulating N\# of users increasing the aggregate load.  
2. **Model Size:** Locked as a static badge for V1. In V2, this becomes a single dropdown `[ Gemma4-31B ]` vs `[ Qwen3.5-397B ]`, which will dynamically update the underlying dataset.  
3. **Cost:** Addressed via the "View Cost-to-Serve" toggle. This is a great example of decision branching: users who only care about raw performance don't need to see cost, but FinOps/Platform Architects can flip the view to see if the extra cost of Managed Lustre is justified by the throughput gains.  
4. **SLOs:** Handled by the interactive "SLO Target Line." This turns a static chart into a calculator. If a user knows their application requires an ITL of \<50ms, they can draw that line and immediately see which storage tier supports the highest concurrency under that constraint.

## 💡 The "Well-Lit Path" Flow

By structuring the UI this way, you guide the user through a narrative:

1. They select a massive **100k Context Length**.  
2. They see the **Baseline** curve immediately crash (OOM).  
3. They toggle on **CPU RAM** and see it survive, but latency spikes early.  
4. They toggle on **Managed Lustre** and see the curve stretch far to the right, maintaining high throughput.  
5. Finally, they open the **Cost** toggle and realize that even though Lustre costs extra, the massive increase in throughput actually *lowers* their Cost per 1M tokens.

This layout restricts the independent variables to just what is necessary to tell that specific story.

# 🗒 Notes

[Sean Horgan](mailto:seanhorgan@google.com), May 7, 2026

# Key Decisions

Some key criteria when deciding between cache offload strategies

* Workload sizes are probably the biggest factor across both individual user workloads and aggregate system workload size, i.e. given N \# of users, how does the sum of these workloads impact system performance?  
* Model size.  
* Cost. Offloading to storage isn’t free of cost as other forms of storage cost money: RAM (CPU RAM) and persistent storage (local SSD, standard cloud storage, managed storage solutions).  
  * Here’s a list of managed storage solutions: lmcache, Lustre  
* SLOs. The primary benefits of offloading is that the system can 1\) support larger inputs, 2\) maintain or increase throughput, all at the expense of adding some latency.  

We need to get real benchmarks in llm-d results store so we can start to experiment with different visualization options. 

From a principles perspective, Prism must aim to **highlight the key independent variables** that drive the primary benefits as measured by the dependent variables. For any given guide we’ll need to constrain the set of independent variables to not overwhelm the user, e.g. 15 dropdowns all in a single UX. Use techniques like like progressive disclosure and decision branching to make the overall UX more approachable.

# Next-level down decisions

Beyond the primary storage solution decisions, there are many configuration options 

## Lustre 

From this post  
[https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/storage-data-transfer/choosing-google-cloud-managed-lustre-for-your-external-kv-cache?e=48754805](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/storage-data-transfer/choosing-google-cloud-managed-lustre-for-your-external-kv-cache?e=48754805)

* Implement direct I/O: Configure your application to access Managed Lustre using the o\_direct flag. This bypasses the general-purpose file system cache, allowing the inference engine to manage the critical host memory more effectively.  
* Tune I/O parallelism: Depending on your inference KV Cache software, its out-of-the-box storage I/O parallelism may not be ideal. You may need to tune the KV Cache software to read KV chunk files with enhanced parallelism to maximize performance. 

At a minimum, we need to include this metadata in benchmarking reports and reproducibility guides.

More key variables listed in [https://github.com/llm-d/llm-d/tree/main/guides/tiered-prefix-cache/storage\#benchmarking](https://github.com/llm-d/llm-d/tree/main/guides/tiered-prefix-cache/storage#benchmarking). 

## LMCache

[https://github.com/lmcache/lmcache](https://github.com/lmcache/lmcache)
